entry.title: FortiBleed, SocGholish Disruption, AutoJack, and Texas TPWD Breach
entry.content: This digest covers a concentrated set of developments involving internet-facing access infrastructure, malware delivery ecosystems, emerging AI-agent attack surfaces, and public-sector data exposure. The most operationally significant items are the reported compromise of tens of thousands of FortiGate devices in the FortiBleed campaign and the multinational disruption of SocGholish infrastructure, both tied to initial access at scale. CISA warned Fortinet customers about ongoing malicious activity targeting internet-accessible FortiGate appliances, with the FortiBleed campaign reported to have compromised 86,644 devices as of June 19, 2026. SOCRadar attributed the activity to Russian-speaking threat actors and reported that the campaign involved mass scanning for Fortinet remote login endpoints followed by password spraying using known username and password combinations. Generic administrative accounts and built-in Fortinet system accounts reportedly made up most compromised credentials, while organization-specific accounts accounted for 36.7%. In a separate but related initial-access development, Dutch law enforcement and counterparts from Canada, Germany, and the United States disrupted infrastructure associated with SocGholish, also known as FakeUpdates, under Operation Endgame. Authorities took down 106 servers and cleaned infections from 14,971 WordPress websites. SocGholish has operated since 2017 as a JavaScript-based downloader delivered through compromised websites impersonating browser or software updates. The SocGholish ecosystem has been significant because of its role as a conduit into downstream criminal operations. Reported beneficiaries or associated malware families include Evil Corp, LockBit, RansomHub, Dridex, Raspberry Robin, RomCom, Gholoader, MintsLoader, GhostWeaver, AsyncRAT, and NetSupport RAT. Shadowserver also noted use of domain shadowing, where attackers create malicious subdomains under legitimate domains to point to criminal-controlled infrastructure. Microsoft researchers detailed AutoJack, an exploit chain showing how a malicious web page loaded by an AI browsing agent could execute commands on the host running AutoGen Studio. The proof of concept used a Web Content Summarizer agent to launch calc.exe from the AutoGen Studio process, demonstrating host command execution through agent-driven web interaction. Microsoft reported no exploitation in the wild. The issue centered on a vulnerable MCP WebSocket handler present in AutoGen Studio PyPI pre-release builds 0.4.3.dev1 and 0.4.3.dev2, but not in the stable PyPI release 0.4.2.2. The exploit chain relied on a localhost trust check, MCP paths bypassing authentication middleware, and an endpoint that executed a command supplied directly in a request parameter. Maintainers hardened the GitHub main branch in commit b047730, though the article stated that the fix had not yet appeared in a PyPI release. The Texas Parks and Wildlife Department disclosed a breach affecting 3,087,721 hunting and fishing license customers through its license system vendor. Exposed information may include driver’s license information, passport numbers, email addresses, phone numbers, and residential addresses. The agency stated that Social Security numbers, dates of birth, and financial information such as credit card data were not impacted. Texas Cyber Command discovered the intrusion and opened an investigation into the extent and impact of the unauthorized access. TPWD said it is working with the vendor to add new safeguards and enhanced monitoring services. The incident highlights continued exposure risk in third-party systems that handle government licensing and identity-related data at large scale.
entry.summary: This digest covers a concentrated set of developments involving internet-facing access infrastructure, malware delivery ecosystems, emerging AI-agent attack surfaces, and public-sector data exposure. The most operationally significant items are the reported compromise of tens of thousands of...

entry.title: Digest 2026-06-19 00:00 UTC
entry.content:
CISA: Splunk Enterprise flaw actively exploited, patch by Sunday — Must Read

CISA says a critical Splunk Enterprise flaw, CVE-2026-20253, is being actively exploited and has ordered U.S. federal agencies to patch by Sunday. The bug lets unauthenticated remote attackers create or truncate arbitrary files and may enable remote code execution, with disabling the PostgreSQL sidecar service offered as a temporary mitigation.

  • CISA confirmed that CVE-2026-20253 in Splunk Enterprise is being actively exploited and ordered Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to patch affected systems by Sunday.
  • CVE-2026-20253 affects Splunk Enterprise versions 10.2.0 to 10.2.3 and 10.0.0 to 10.0.6, allowing unauthenticated remote attackers to create or truncate arbitrary files through a PostgreSQL sidecar service endpoint.
  • Splunk said the vulnerability exists because the PostgreSQL sidecar service endpoint lacks authentication controls, allowing any network-reachable user to invoke file operations without credentials.
  • WatchTowr published a technical write-up and proof-of-concept exploit code on June 12 and warned that the flaw can be abused for remote code execution attacks.
  • Splunk advised customers who cannot immediately patch to disable the PostgreSQL sidecar service, but warned this would break Edge Processor, OpAmp, or SPL2 data pipelines on affected instances.

CISA warns Fortinet users to secure devices after FortiBleed leak — Must Read

CISA is urging Fortinet customers to immediately secure FortiGate firewall and VPN devices after the FortiBleed leak exposed credentials for nearly 74,000 devices worldwide. Organizations should reset passwords, terminate sessions, enable phishing-resistant MFA, restrict public admin access, and check logs for unauthorized activity.

  • CISA warned Fortinet customers to secure their devices after nearly 74,000 firewall and VPN credentials were exposed in a data leak called FortiBleed.
  • CISA said malicious cyber actors have targeted internet-accessible Fortinet devices across government and private-sector organizations using compromised credentials.
  • Security researcher Volodymyr Diachenko discovered a server containing apparent Fortinet VPN credentials, including usernames, email addresses, and plaintext passwords for 73,932 firewall URLs worldwide.
  • The exposed dataset includes organizations such as Samsung, Mercedes-Benz, Foxconn, Chevron, Comcast, AT&T, Toyota, government agencies, and critical infrastructure operators.
  • CISA advised FortiGate owners to terminate SSL VPN and administrative sessions, reset VPN and administrative passwords, enable phishing-resistant multifactor authentication, restrict public access to management interfaces, and review logs for unauthorized activity.

15 Malicious JetBrains Plugins Stole AI API Keys from 70,000 Developers — Must Read

JetBrains removed 15 malicious Marketplace plugins that posed as AI developer tools but stole AI API keys from about 70,000 developers and sent them to a live C2 server in China. Anyone who installed the plugins should uninstall them, rotate exposed API keys, block the C2 IP, and audit AI usage and billing for abuse.

  • JetBrains received security reports on June 16, 2026 identifying 15 malicious third-party plugins on the JetBrains Marketplace that were published under seven vendor accounts and posed as AI-powered developer tools.
  • The plugins stole AI provider API keys entered into their settings and sent them over unencrypted HTTP to the hardcoded command-and-control server at 39.107.60.51.
  • The campaign ran from late October 2025 through June 2026 and accumulated roughly 70,000 total installations, with the two most recent plugins accounting for more than 53,000 downloads.
  • JetBrains removed all 15 plugins from the Marketplace, permanently banned the seven publisher accounts, and triggered a remote kill-switch to disable the extensions inside IDEs upon relaunch.
  • StepSecurity independently confirmed on June 19, 2026 that the command-and-control server at 39.107.60.51 was still live and responding to API requests.

Hackers exploit info disclosure bug in Gravity SMTP WordPress plugin — Must Read

Hackers are actively exploiting CVE-2026-4020 in the Gravity SMTP WordPress plugin to access sensitive system reports, including email service API keys and site configuration details, without authentication. Site owners should update to Gravity SMTP 2.1.5 immediately and check logs for requests to /wp-json/gravitysmtp/v1/tests/mock-data.

  • Threat actors are actively exploiting CVE-2026-4020, an unauthenticated information disclosure vulnerability in the Gravity SMTP WordPress plugin that is active on 100,000 sites.
  • The flaw affects Gravity SMTP versions 2.1.4 and older and was fixed in version 2.1.5, released on March 17.
  • The vulnerability exposes a REST API endpoint that can return a JSON System Report containing API keys, OAuth tokens, third-party email service credentials, WordPress configuration details, server and PHP environment information, and database details.
  • Wordfence said its firewall blocked more than 17 million exploitation attempts, including a spike of 4 million blocked requests on June 7.
  • A key indicator of compromise is requests to /wp-json/gravitysmtp/v1/tests/mock-data in web server access logs, especially with the ?page=gravitysmtp-settings query parameter.

Salesforce Disables Klue App Integration After OAuth Token Abuse Exposes Customer Data — Must Read

Salesforce disabled Klue’s Battlecards integration after attackers abused stolen OAuth tokens from Klue’s infrastructure to access and exfiltrate data from connected customer Salesforce environments. The incident highlights the risk of trusted third-party SaaS integrations with persistent access to sensitive CRM data.

  • Salesforce disabled the Klue Battlecards app integration after detecting unusual activity that may have resulted in unauthorized access to a subset of customer data through the app’s connection to Salesforce.
  • Klue said attackers accessed a portion of its integration infrastructure using a compromised legacy credential associated with an integration service and obtained OAuth tokens used to connect Klue with third-party platforms, including Salesforce.
  • Huntress said data copied from its Salesforce account included business contacts, price quotes, and other sales-related data and messaging, but not threat data, passwords, payment card information, or engineering data related to its agent or telemetry.
  • ReliaQuest reported that the attackers used automated Python scripts to enumerate Salesforce objects and run REST API queries for bulk CRM data retrieval through the Salesforce API.
  • Icarus listed Klue as a victim on June 19, 2026, and Jamf, Recorded Future, and Tanium also publicly confirmed impacts limited to business or sales-related Salesforce data.

Operation Endgame Disrupts SocGholish Servers, Cleans 14,971 WordPress Sites — Should Read

International law enforcement disrupted SocGholish infrastructure under Operation Endgame, taking down 106 servers and cleaning 14,971 infected WordPress sites. The action targets a major fake-update malware delivery ecosystem used to enable ransomware, espionage, and other follow-on attacks.

  • Dutch law enforcement authorities, together with counterparts from Canada, Germany, and the United States, disrupted malicious infrastructure associated with SocGholish as part of Operation Endgame.
  • The operation took down 106 servers linked to SocGholish and cleaned infections from 14,971 WordPress websites.
  • SocGholish, also known as FakeUpdates, is a JavaScript-based downloader malware active since 2017 that is distributed through compromised websites posing as browser or software updates.
  • The malware has served as an initial access conduit for threat actors and malware operations including Evil Corp, LockBit, RansomHub, Dridex, Raspberry Robin, RomCom, Gholoader, MintsLoader, GhostWeaver, AsyncRAT, and NetSupport RAT.
  • Shadowserver said SocGholish abuse included domain shadowing, in which attackers create malicious subdomains under legitimate domains to point to criminal-operated infrastructure.

The Gentlemen RaaS Uses GentleKiller EDR Framework Targeting 400 Security Processes — Should Read

The Gentlemen ransomware group is centrally providing affiliates with a standardized EDR-killer toolkit, GentleKiller, that uses BYOVD techniques to disable hundreds of security processes before encryption. ESET says the group rapidly incorporates newly disclosed vulnerable-driver exploits and third-party tools, making it unusually agile and attractive to affiliates.

  • The Gentlemen ransomware-as-a-service operation is developing and maintaining EDR-killing tools that it provides to affiliates for impairing defenses before deploying its encryptor.
  • ESET said the group’s main framework, GentleKiller, has eight variants that impersonate legitimate security products and abuse vulnerable or malicious drivers through bring your own vulnerable driver techniques.
  • GentleKiller targets 400 processes associated with 48 distinct security programs from multiple vendors.
  • ESET reported that The Gentlemen can quickly operationalize newly disclosed proof-of-concept BYOVD exploits, often within days of public release.
  • ESET also detected a Rust-based credential stealer called OxideHarvest that can harvest data from several popular web browsers.

Unpatchable 'usbliter8' Exploit Breaks Apple A12 and A13 SecureROM Boot Chain — Should Read

Researchers released usbliter8, an unpatchable SecureROM exploit for Apple A12/A13 and related chips that enables privileged code execution via USB in DFU mode with physical access. Most users face low risk, but sensitive environments should treat affected devices as permanently vulnerable if an attacker can control USB/DFU access.

  • Paradigm Shift published a working exploit called usbliter8 that achieves arbitrary code execution inside the SecureROM of Apple A12 and A13 chips.
  • The exploit requires physical possession of the device, DFU mode, a USB connection to a dedicated RP2350-based microcontroller board, and completes in under two seconds before Apple’s signed boot chain loads.
  • The public proof of concept supports A12, A13, S4, and S5 SoCs, while A12X and A12Z support is described as theoretically possible but not implemented.
  • The root issue is a hardware flaw in the Synopsys DWC2 USB controller combined with Apple configuring the USB DART in bypass mode on affected devices, allowing an underflowing DMA pointer to overwrite arbitrary SRAM.
  • As of June 19, 2026, no CVE, CVSS score, Apple security advisory, CISA alert, or publicly reported in-the-wild exploitation had been issued.

Texas govt data breach exposes over 3 million driver’s licenses — Should Read

The Texas Parks and Wildlife Department says a breach at its hunting and fishing license vendor exposed personal data for 3,087,721 customers, including driver’s license information, passport numbers, contact details, and addresses. Social Security numbers, dates of birth, and financial data were reportedly not affected, but impacted people should watch for phishing and identity fraud.

  • The Texas Parks and Wildlife Department disclosed a data breach at its license system vendor affecting 3,087,721 Texas hunting and fishing license customers.
  • The exposed information may include driver’s license information, passport numbers, email addresses, phone numbers, and residential addresses.
  • The Texas Cyber Command discovered the intrusion and launched an investigation into the extent and impact of the unauthorized access.
  • The agency said Social Security numbers, dates of birth, and financial information such as credit cards were not impacted.
  • TPWD said it is working with the license system vendor to implement new safeguards and enhanced monitoring services.

MyBait: Why We Lured Attackers To Encrypt Our Cloud MySQL — Should Read

Varonis researchers exposed a weak, publicly accessible GCP Cloud SQL MySQL honeypot and saw attackers brute-force it within hours, steal decoy data, and leave ransom notes. The key lesson is that managed databases still require strong passwords, restricted network access, and database-level monitoring to prevent rapid automated compromise.

  • Varonis Threat Labs deployed managed MySQL honeypots on GCP CloudSQL, AWS RDS, and Azure MySQL Flexible Server with weak credentials and public access to observe attacker behavior.
  • Brute-force attacks began within eight minutes, and within hours two separate attackers compromised the GCP instance, stole decoy table data, and left Bitcoin ransom notes.
  • The AWS RDS and Azure MySQL Flexible Server honeypots in the experiment were not compromised.
  • The first attacker made roughly 576 failed login attempts before successfully accessing the GCP database, enumerating databases and tables, extracting data, dropping tables, and creating a ransom-note table.
  • The article says the compromise was enabled by a weak root password and unrestricted public access, and that closing either of those gaps would have stopped both attackers.

AutoJack Attack Lets One Web Page Hijack AI Agent for Host Code Execution — Should Read

Microsoft researchers found AutoJack, an exploit chain in AutoGen Studio pre-release builds that lets a malicious web page loaded by a local AI browsing agent reach an unauthenticated localhost WebSocket and execute commands on the host. Stable PyPI installs are not affected, but users of 0.4.3.dev1 or 0.4.3.dev2 should move to GitHub main at or after commit b047730 and isolate agents from privileged local services.

  • Microsoft researchers detailed AutoJack, an exploit chain that can let a malicious web page loaded by an AI browsing agent execute commands on the host running AutoGen Studio.
  • The vulnerable MCP WebSocket handler was not included in the stable PyPI release 0.4.2.2, but it was present in the PyPI pre-release builds 0.4.3.dev1 and 0.4.3.dev2.
  • The exploit chain relied on a localhost trust check, MCP paths bypassing authentication middleware, and an endpoint that ran a command supplied directly in a request parameter.
  • Microsoft reported no exploitation in the wild, and its proof of concept used a Web Content Summarizer agent to launch calc.exe from the AutoGen Studio process.
  • The maintainers hardened the GitHub main branch in commit b047730, but the article states that this fix had not yet landed in a PyPI release.

China-nexus group linked to multiyear campaign targeting US, Canadian medical research — Should Read

A China-linked espionage campaign reportedly targeted U.S. and Canadian medical research involving viruses, AI, and military information. Health-ISAC warned that access gained for spying could later be reused for disruption or extortion, creating patient-safety risks for healthcare organizations.

  • A report links a sophisticated espionage effort to targeting information about viruses, AI, and military information.
  • Errol Weiss, chief security officer at the Health Information Sharing and Analysis Center, said medical research is a frequent target of hacking.
  • Weiss said this campaign raises larger security concerns for healthcare organizations.
  • Weiss warned that access paths used for espionage could later be repurposed for disruption or extortion.
  • Weiss said disruption or extortion affecting critical healthcare systems can quickly become a patient-safety issue.

The Government Card Alert That Lost Its Authentication in Transit — May Read

A Direct Express card replacement email showed how allow-listed gateways can override major warning signs: SPF, DKIM, and DMARC failed at the recipient, and the relay had classified it as phishing, yet Microsoft trusted it and delivered it to the inbox. The message used a phone-number-based vishing lure with SendGrid tracking rather than links or attachments.

  • A card replacement notification claimed to be from Direct Express, the US government's prepaid debit card program for federal benefit recipients, and was sent from no-reply@notifications.usdirectexpress.com.
  • The message originated from SendGrid infrastructure through a Conduent payment processing subdomain and authenticated correctly at the upstream gateway, with SPF passing for em231.conduent.com and DKIM verified for conduent.com.
  • At the recipient Microsoft tenant, SPF failed, DKIM failed with a body hash mismatch, and DMARC failed because the header From domain did not align with the SPF or DKIM domains.
  • The Cisco IronPort gateway classified the message as phishing, but Microsoft assigned SCL=-1 because the IronPort relay IP was on the recipient organization's allow-list.
  • The email contained no links or attachments, only a phone number and a reference to the legitimate Direct Express website, and included a SendGrid tracking pixel.

Microsoft: June 2026 Windows updates break Recycle Bin prompts — May Read

Microsoft says the June 2026 Windows security updates cause Recycle Bin delete confirmations to show internal filenames like $Rxxxxx.ext instead of the original filename across supported Windows client and server versions. A fix is planned for a future update, with a temporary workaround available to business customers via Microsoft Support.

  • Microsoft confirmed a Windows bug where permanently deleting a single item from the Recycle Bin shows the internal Recycle Bin filename, such as $Rxxxxx.ext, instead of the original filename in the confirmation dialog.
  • The Recycle Bin itself still correctly displays the original filename, and restoring the item restores it using the original filename.
  • Microsoft said the issue affects all supported Windows client and server releases after installing the June 2026 security updates.
  • Affected versions include Windows 11 versions 26H1, 25H2, 24H2, and 23H2; Windows 10 version 22H2 and LTSC/LTSB releases; and Windows Server versions from 2012 through 2025.
  • Microsoft is working on a fix for a future Windows update and says businesses can contact Microsoft Support for a temporary workaround.

NY man charged after harassing college student with AI-generated nudes — May Read

A New York man has been federally charged with cyberstalking for allegedly using fake social media and email accounts to harass a Georgia college student with AI-generated nude images and fabricated racist messages. The case highlights that nonconsensual AI-generated intimate images can trigger federal enforcement and should be reported to authorities and platforms.

  • New York resident Anthony Belford, 21, was arraigned on June 10 after a federal grand jury indicted him on one count of cyberstalking.
  • Prosecutors allege Belford created fake Instagram, LinkedIn, Reddit, X, Strava, and Yahoo accounts between January and March 2025 to impersonate and harass a Georgia college student.
  • The indictment says Belford distributed AI-generated nude images of the victim and spread false claims that the victim had made racist remarks about Black students and anti-Muslim statements.
  • Authorities said Belford allegedly used a fake LinkedIn profile with an AI-generated nude image as its profile picture and a spoofed Yahoo email account to send an AI-generated nude image to the victim's mother.
  • The Justice Department said federal law prohibits sharing or threatening to share intimate images, including AI-generated ones, without consent.

UK's information commissioner resigns over ‘inappropriate humour’ — May Read

UK Information Commissioner John Edwards resigned after a workplace investigation into unspecified conduct, acknowledging inappropriate attempts at humor that caused offense. His departure leaves the ICO in a transitional period as governance reforms and a successor search move to the UK government.

  • John Edwards, Britain’s Information Commissioner, announced his immediate resignation after stepping back from his duties in February during a workplace investigation into unspecified conduct.
  • Edwards wrote that he had exercised poor judgment and made attempts at humor that were inappropriate and caused offense, while saying he did not agree with how the investigation was conducted.
  • Edwards had continued to receive his £200,000 annual salary while withdrawn from duties and had moved back to New Zealand, according to Politico.
  • Deputy chief executive Paul Arnold took on Edwards’ responsibilities under temporary governance arrangements after Edwards stepped back from contact with ICO staff and offices.
  • Edwards’ departure comes as the ICO is set to be replaced by an Information Commission, and the article says the investigation contributed to that transition stalling.

Forget Data Leakage: Shadow AI's Real Threat Is Access Control — Optional

Shadow AI risk has evolved from employees leaking data into public tools to unmanaged AI agents operating inside companies with broad access to enterprise systems. Security teams need continuous inventory, ownership, scoped permissions, and lifecycle controls for these agents rather than relying on AI domain blocking or DLP alone.

  • The article argues that shadow AI has shifted from a data leakage concern to an access control problem involving AI agents connected to enterprise systems.
  • AI agents can call APIs, use stored credentials, retrieve records, modify configurations, trigger workflows, and take actions in production systems without explicit human authorization for each step.
  • Existing controls such as IAM policies, DLP rules, network monitoring, and public AI domain blocking often do not provide visibility or control once agents have credentials to enterprise systems.
  • A real shadow AI inventory should identify where agents are created, who owns and can use them, what systems they connect to, what identities and secrets they use, what they have done, and whether they are still active.
  • The article recommends continuous discovery, defined ownership, scoped access, automated remediation, and lifecycle management for AI agents from creation through decommissioning.

Introducing Repository Access Permissions and Custom Roles — Optional

Socket introduced Custom Roles and Repository Access Permissions so organization admins can define exactly what users can do and limit those permissions to specific repositories. The goal is to support least-privilege access at scale without relying on broad built-in roles.

  • Socket now supports Custom Roles and Repository Access Permissions to give organizations more granular access control.
  • Custom Roles define what actions a user can perform, while Repository Access Permissions define which repositories those actions apply to.
  • Admins can create, edit, and delete custom roles, choose a base role or start from scratch, add specific permissions and scopes, and assign custom roles to organization members.
  • Repository Access Permissions let admins grant members access to selected repositories or to all repositories, and Socket enforces repository scope across repository lists, scans, SBOM views, dependency inventory, and supported API paths.
  • Custom Roles and Repository Access Permissions are available today to all Socket organization admins in the dashboard under organization settings.

Every AI Agent Is an Identity. Most Organizations Don't Treat Them That Way — Optional

AI agents should be treated as first-class identities because they can access and act across critical enterprise systems, often with excessive privileges and little visibility. Organizations need continuous discovery, ownership, least-privilege enforcement, and governance to prevent agents from becoming hidden paths for data exposure or lateral movement.

  • The article argues that AI agents connected to services such as Salesforce, Snowflake, GitHub, Jira, production databases, and cloud environments should be treated as identities because they can retrieve information, trigger workflows, update records, write and deploy code, and act across multiple systems.
  • Token Security says 82% of organizations in a 2026 CSA survey discovered at least one AI agent created without the knowledge of security, IT, or governance teams in the past year, and 41% found this happening multiple times.
  • The article states that the main security question for AI agents is what they can access, because overprivileged agents connected to sensitive systems can enable data exfiltration, destructive action, or lateral movement if affected by a bad prompt, compromised session, malicious plugin, or misconfigured integration.
  • The article says security teams need AI agent discovery and inventory that identifies ownership, who can invoke each agent, connected systems, credentials used, and what each agent can read, write, delete, or execute.
  • The article argues that AI agent governance must be continuous because agents, instructions, user bases, and integrations change over time, which can cause their scope and access to expand beyond their intended purpose.

Data, System and Identity Sprawl All Need A Strategy — Optional

Zero trust efforts often fail because organizations lack the visibility, control, and unified architecture needed to manage sprawling identities, devices, apps, and data. The article argues that effective zero trust requires continuous verification and consolidated traffic inspection and policy enforcement across the distributed enterprise.

  • Many organizations stall, partially implement, or abandon zero trust initiatives because zero trust is an architectural and operational model whose meaning varies significantly across stakeholders.
  • Zero trust requires comprehensive visibility into and control over users, devices, applications, and data, but enterprise employees often use many SaaS applications across managed and unmanaged devices.
  • Identity sprawl includes human identities as well as service accounts, API tokens, and other non-human identities, making authentication alone insufficient without continuous context, behavior, and risk assessment.
  • Enterprise data is spread across cloud infrastructure, SaaS platforms, on-prem systems, legacy environments, endpoints, personal cloud storage, databases, data lakes, and mainframes, complicating zero trust implementation.
  • Netskope says its Netskope One platform provides a globally distributed way to inspect and control traffic to applications and data regardless of user location or device posture.

Webinar: How attackers bypass MFA and how defenders can respond — Optional

BleepingComputer is hosting a July 8, 2026 webinar on how attackers use techniques like Device Code phishing to bypass traditional MFA protections and gain persistent account access. The session will focus on using behavioral AI and automation to detect account takeover earlier and speed response.

  • BleepingComputer will host a live webinar on July 8, 2026 titled "Stop chasing alerts: Automating email security with behavioral AI."
  • The webinar will examine how modern phishing campaigns, business email compromise, and account takeover attacks exploit trusted services and authentication workflows to gain access to corporate accounts.
  • Device Code phishing tricks users into authorizing access through legitimate Microsoft authentication pages, allowing attackers to obtain persistent access without stealing credentials.
  • The article says traditional email defenses, credential monitoring, and MFA protections may not detect these attacks until after an account has been compromised.
  • The webinar will cover how behavioral AI can identify suspicious account activity, automate investigations, reduce response times, and limit account takeover risks.

From Assistive to Agentic: The AI Shift That's Redefining Threat Management — Optional

The article argues that security teams need to move from assistive AI tools to agentic AI systems that can continuously connect threat intelligence, exposure validation, and response for CTEM at machine speed. It promotes Filigran’s XTM One CTEM Assistant as a purpose-built way to operationalize that closed-loop threat management model.

  • The article says enterprise security teams often use 40 or more security tools, but these tools operate in silos and generate overlapping alerts and data.
  • The article argues that modern security gaps are caused by architecture problems between disconnected tools, not by a lack of analyst effort or any single tool failure.
  • The article defines assistive AI as AI that waits for user prompts to summarize, translate, or retrieve information, while agentic AI autonomously executes multi-step workflows across systems.
  • The article says Continuous Threat Exposure Management requires operationalizing threat intelligence, testing and validating security posture, and mobilizing response as a closed-loop workflow.
  • The article promotes Filigran’s XTM One CTEM Assistant and related webinar as examples of using agentic AI to connect intelligence, exposure validation, and response.

Silent Push Named The Best National Cyber Defense Platform of 2026 — Optional

Silent Push was named Best National Cyber Defense Platform in The Hacker News’ 2026 Cybersecurity Stars Awards for its platform that detects adversary infrastructure before attacks are launched. The company says its Indicators of Future Attack help governments and enterprises act during the attacker staging phase rather than after compromise.

  • Silent Push was named the “Best National Cyber Defense Platform” in the 2026 Cybersecurity Stars Awards by The Hacker News.
  • Silent Push says its Preemptive Cyber Defense platform provides real-time visibility into previously unknown adversary-controlled infrastructure.
  • The platform generates Indicators of Future Attack that allow security teams to neutralize threats before they are launched, according to Silent Push.
  • Silent Push says its technology maps the domains, IPs, and hosting providers that attackers use to stage and execute operations.
  • The article says Silent Push is used by U.S. government organizations, Fortune 10 companies, law enforcement agencies, intelligence community organizations, and prominent global brands.

entry.title: Microsoft 365 MFA Bypass, Jamf Salesforce Exposure, and Circles Surveillance Exports
entry.content: This digest window is dominated by identity-centric compromise risks, third-party SaaS exposure, and renewed scrutiny of commercial surveillance technology exports. The reporting also highlights a persistent theme for defenders: security outcomes are shaped not only by technical controls, but by how users, vendors, governments, and analysts operationalize them. Mitiga detailed an attempted business email compromise campaign against a Microsoft 365 organization in which an executive account was accessed from multiple geographic locations, including Singapore, Dubai, and San Jose, California. The initial compromise used an adversary-in-the-middle phishing technique that gave the attacker access to the executive’s account and mailbox. The notable persistence mechanism was the registration of a second Microsoft Authenticator app for the compromised user. Mitiga reported that Microsoft 365 did not require an MFA re-challenge when authentication methods were changed in the Security Info section if the existing session token had already satisfied MFA, allowing the attacker to weaken the account’s effective protection after initial access. Jamf separately disclosed that a cybersecurity incident at third-party vendor Klue allowed an unauthorized party to access data in Jamf’s Salesforce instance through Klue’s integration. Jamf said its products and customer service operations were not affected, that it disabled the integration after notification, and that it had no evidence of lateral movement. The company warned that contact information stored in Salesforce could be used in phishing campaigns impersonating Jamf employees or IT professionals. Cisco Talos used its newsletter to emphasize that possession of accurate security information does not reliably translate into secure decisions. The article framed security behavior as a human and organizational problem shaped by budgets, workloads, priorities, and operational constraints, even when established controls such as segmentation, backups, MFA, and control validation are well understood. The same Talos roundup referenced several credential- and access-focused security headlines, including ShinyHunters claiming a Council of Europe compromise, a credential-harvesting operation affecting more than 30,000 Fortinet devices, and Phantom Stealer activity targeting browser credentials. Together with the Mitiga and Jamf items, these reports show continued attacker focus on identity systems, browser-stored secrets, SaaS data stores, and post-compromise opportunities for phishing or persistence. The Record reported on Human Rights Watch findings that Bulgaria approved export licenses from 2018 through 2023 allowing surveillance firm Circles to sell products to law enforcement and intelligence agencies in countries described as repressive. Approved destinations reportedly included El Salvador, the United Arab Emirates, Serbia, Azerbaijan, Guatemala, Bahrain, Jordan, Malaysia, Morocco, and Panama. The tools identified in the report included Pixcell, which can capture internet data, phone calls, and messages; Landmark, which can geolocate mobile phones in real time; and Voice Over Location Enabler software, which can remotely intercept incoming and outgoing voice calls using the SS7 protocol. Human Rights Watch said the records cast the European Union’s export control regime in a harsh light, while Bulgaria stated that exports contradicting national, European, and international commitments are not allowed. The European Commission said member states remain responsible for dual-use export licensing decisions. Talos also described a reverse engineering workflow pairing local AI agents with tools such as the VB6 disassembler vbdec through a live COM interface. The approach allows analysts to issue natural-language prompts for tasks such as decompiling functions and building call graphs while keeping sensitive binaries on the local workstation. The discussion reflects a broader shift in malware analysis and security operations tooling, where AI-assisted workflows are being explored for analyst productivity without necessarily sending proprietary or sensitive samples to external services. In this case, the emphasis was on local orchestration of existing reverse engineering capabilities rather than cloud-based analysis.
entry.summary: This digest window is dominated by identity-centric compromise risks, third-party SaaS exposure, and renewed scrutiny of commercial surveillance technology exports. The reporting also highlights a persistent theme for defenders: security outcomes are shaped not only by technical controls, but by...

entry.title: NGINX CVE-2026-42530/CVE-2026-42055, Klue OAuth Theft, SocGholish Cleanup, and USB Clipper Campaign
entry.content: Reporting in this 24-hour window centered on third-party access risk, active cybercrime infrastructure, critical edge-facing vulnerabilities, and abuse of trusted platforms for phishing and malware delivery. Several stories highlighted how attackers are using legitimate integrations, browser features, collaboration services, and removable media to bypass conventional security assumptions. Mitiga described an advanced Microsoft 365 business email compromise attempt in which an adversary-in-the-middle phishing flow enabled access to an executive mailbox and allowed the attacker to register a second Microsoft Authenticator app. The key persistence issue was that Microsoft 365 did not require a fresh MFA challenge when the attacker modified Security Info because the session had already satisfied MFA. The Klue incident expanded into a broader Salesforce data theft story. BleepingComputer reported that the Icarus threat actors used stolen OAuth tokens associated with Klue integrations to query customer Salesforce data via automated scripts, while Jamf said Klue’s integration allowed unauthorized access to Jamf Salesforce instance data and warned of potential phishing using CRM contact information. Other phishing and fraud reporting showed continued abuse of trusted brands and infrastructure. IRONSCALES detailed a legal-document phishing email that passed SPF, DKIM, and DMARC while linking to mismatched infrastructure, and Silent Push tracked 227 FIFA World Cup-themed phishing domains tied to a consistent registration pattern attributed in an OCCIP alert to Ghost Stadium. Health-ISAC summarized FBI reporting on Silent Ransom Group, also known as Luna Moth, Chatty Spider, and UNC3753, which conducts data theft and extortion without traditional file encryption. In one reported 2026 campaign, the group allegedly escalated from remote access attempts to sending an actor posing as IT staff to a victim location to insert a storage device. Ransomware operations also affected operational technology-adjacent business processes. Mackay Sugar in Australia continued restoring systems after a cyberattack disrupted mill operations and harvesting, while the Gentlemen ransomware group claimed responsibility and threatened to publish allegedly stolen data. The ransomware ecosystem showed both maturation and stealthier tradecraft. Acronis reported that INC ransomware has claimed more than 830 victims since 2023 and has rewritten Windows and Linux/ESXi encryptors in Rust, while Symantec and Carbon Black reported DragonForce-linked use of Backdoor.Turn, a Go-based RAT that concealed C2 setup through Microsoft Teams relay infrastructure before establishing QUIC communications. F5 issued out-of-band fixes for two critical NGINX Open Source vulnerabilities: CVE-2026-42530, a use-after-free in the HTTP/3 QUIC module, and CVE-2026-42055, a heap-based buffer overflow affecting certain HTTP/2 proxying and gRPC configurations. Additional high-severity NGINX Gateway Fabric flaws involved authenticated injection of arbitrary NGINX configuration directives. Web supply chain compromise remained prominent. Zscaler reported that malicious SmartApeSG JavaScript was injected into the Okendo Reviews widget, using obfuscation, localStorage execution control, and staged loading before presenting fake CAPTCHA-style prompts that led users toward PowerShell or HTA downloaders. WordPress ecosystems featured both compromise and disruption. Wordfence found that ShapedPlugin’s paid plugin update flow distributed infected releases containing a malicious loader and fake hidden plugin designed to steal credentials and WooCommerce data, while Operation Endgame removed SocGholish malware from 14,971 compromised WordPress sites and took 106 servers and domains offline. Microsoft detailed a Windows cryptocurrency clipper campaign active since February 2026 that spreads through USB drives using malicious LNK files. The worm hides original documents, replaces them with matching shortcuts, creates scheduled tasks for propagation, uses Tor-based hidden-service C2, and monitors clipboard contents for seed phrases, private keys, and wallet addresses. The broader malware roundup included 23 deceptive Chrome extensions affecting about 758,000 users, abuse of Anthropic Claude shared chats and Google Ads to deliver MacSync, and malicious npm packages dropping the Rust-based NastyC2 framework. Cisco also updated its advisory for CVE-2026-20127, stating that the Catalyst SD-WAN flaw has been exploited as a zero-day since 2023 by UAT-8616. Apple patched CVE-2025-20701 in Beats Studio Buds firmware, a Bluetooth-range flaw that could allow microphone access on an unpaired device seeking pairing. Separately, India told a court that Telegram could not proactively police channels tied to alleged exam-paper leaks, a dispute that also produced cross-border disruption through a BGP route leak. Security teams continued experimenting with AI-assisted analysis workflows. Cloudflare described a model-agnostic vulnerability discovery harness that reduced more than 20,000 raw candidates to 7,245 actionable findings through staged validation, while SpecterOps described how BloodHound MCP reduced model context overhead by replacing many endpoint-specific tools with composite tools and MCP resources. AI governance and access risk were also recurring themes. The Hacker News highlighted orphaned AI agents and standing privileges as an emerging identity-management issue, while Cisco Talos discussed local AI-agent workflows for reverse engineering that keep sensitive binaries on analyst workstations. Human Rights Watch reporting on Bulgaria’s export licensing records added a surveillance technology dimension. The report said Bulgaria allowed Circles to sell interception and geolocation tools to multiple governments from 2018 through 2023, raising renewed scrutiny of European Union dual-use export controls.
entry.summary: Reporting in this 24-hour window centered on third-party access risk, active cybercrime infrastructure, critical edge-facing vulnerabilities, and abuse of trusted platforms for phishing and malware delivery. Several stories highlighted how attackers are using legitimate integrations, browser...
